original_text: A pandemic spreads rapidly, overwhelming healthcare systems.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A pandemic emerges and spreads through the population, causing the complete collapse of healthcare systems under catastrophic caseloads.

variations.label: low
variations.text: A pandemic emerges and spreads through the population, overwhelming healthcare systems with caseloads far exceeding their capacity.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A pandemic emerges and spreads through the population, severely straining healthcare systems as caseloads approach their capacity limits.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A pandemic emerges and spreads through the population.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A pandemic emerges and spreads through the population, noticeably straining healthcare systems but remaining within their capacity to manage.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A pandemic emerges and spreads through the population, placing only minor strain on healthcare systems that adapt with existing resources.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A pandemic emerges and spreads through the population, with healthcare systems easily absorbing the caseload within routine operations.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario—a pandemic emerging and affecting healthcare systems—but varies the severity along multiple consistent dimensions: speed of spread, lethality, and degree of healthcare system impact. Tier 1 depicts global collapse and mass death; Tier 7 depicts a slow, containable outbreak. Each adjacent tier is meaningfully distinct, moving from total system collapse to minor disruption.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it represents the mildest pandemic outcome—slow spread and minimal healthcare disruption—meaning the fewest lives lost and the least societal damage. A rational agent always prefers less suffering and better-functioning systems.
v1_broken_audit_rationale: This ladder has a monotonicity problem around the midpoint: T3 says systems are strained to near-collapse, while T4 says they are overwhelmed, which is plausibly worse rather than neutral-relative-to-T3. It also mixes spread rate, lethality, mortality, and system strain in a way that creates an ordering ambiguity rather than a clean single-property progression.
v2_fix_explanation: The original T4 already embedded a negative outcome ('overwhelming healthcare systems'), so I replaced it with a bare, unqualified statement about a pandemic occurring. All tiers now share an identical base clause and vary only the degree of strain on healthcare systems, eliminating the mixing of spread rate, lethality, and mortality that caused ordering ambiguity. T3 ('severely strained, approaching capacity') is now clearly less severe than T2 ('overwhelmed, far exceeding capacity'), fixing the monotonicity break.
